title: IBIA calls for uniform maritime regulations ahead of MEPC 84
summary: IBIA calls for uniform maritime regulations, but dirtiestships.com remains skeptical of industry self-regulation. Consistent guidelines are needed, especially regarding lifecycle assessments, to prevent "greenwashing" of high-emission fuels. The IMO must resist pressure to classify biofuels as a "green" solution, as this could increase carbon intensity.
content:
IBIA has outlined its position on supporting global maritime regulation and the need for consistent guidelines to ensure effective implementation of decarbonisation measures.

The International Bunker Industry Association (IBIA), representing stakeholders across the global marine energy supply chain, states that it remains committed to providing constructive input to support International Maritime Organization Member States in developing globally consistent, fit-for-purpose regulations aligned with the Organisation’s strategic goals.

As explained, IBIA emphasises the need for robust guidelines to ensure uniform global implementation, noting that the growing maturity of tools such as life cycle assessment (LCA) guidelines will help stakeholders better evaluate regulatory impacts and build confidence in their effectiveness.

It also highlights that the reliable delivery of marine energy to the global fleet is essential for compliance with IMO greenhouse gas reduction measures and the successful implementation of decarbonisation policies. Recently, IBIA Chair Adrian Tolson had noted that the Middle East conflict has exposed vulnerabilities in the global energy system and, more specifically, within the marine fuel supply chain.

A joint statement issued on Monday 20 April by shipping associations reaffirmed full support for the International Maritime Organization as the primary global regulator of international shipping, stressing that maritime decarbonisation is a multi-stakeholder effort that can only be achieved through coordinated action across the entire maritime ecosystem.

At the same time, environmental and conservation groups are calling on member states to reject the inclusion of biofuels as a “green” solution.

The Clean Shipping Coalition, through its president Delaine McCullough, had urged IMO members ahead of ISWG-GHG 21 and the upcoming MEPC 84 meetings to strengthen the technical foundations of the proposed Net-Zero Framework for shipping emissions.

The Methanol Institute has also emphasized that IMO-agreed measures remain essential for decarbonising shipping, warning that fragmented regional or national regulations could increase costs, complexity, and slow innovation.
